name: Mount Nemrut
description: Mount Nemrut, located in southeastern Turkey, is a mountain steeped in history and mythology. It is most famous for the colossal statues adorning its summit, built by King Antiochus I Theos of Commagene (69–34 B.C.E.) as a tomb-sanctuary. These monumental sculptures, depicting Antiochus himself, various Greek, Armenian, and Persian deities, lions, eagles, and other symbolic figures, represent a syncretistic religious belief system blending Greek, Persian, and local Anatolian traditions. The mountain was believed to be a sacred space, a direct connection between the earthly realm and the divine. The site served not only as a royal tomb but also as a stage for elaborate religious ceremonies and processions. The alignment of the statues with the rising and setting sun suggests a sophisticated understanding of astronomy and a deep reverence for the sun god. The sheer scale and artistic ambition of the site reflect the wealth and power of the Commagene kingdom and its desire to create a lasting legacy. Today, Mount Nemrut stands as a testament to the cultural exchange and religious syncretism that characterized the Hellenistic period in Anatolia. Its remote location and dramatic landscape contribute to its aura of mystery and sacredness, drawing visitors from around the world to witness this remarkable blend of art, religion, and history. Its crumbling statues are a poignant reminder of the impermanence of earthly power and the enduring power of myth and legend. The mountain continues to be a site of archaeological exploration and scholarly interest, revealing new insights into the Commagene kingdom and its place in the broader history of the region. The Eastern and Western terraces, with their respective statues, embody the dual nature of the king's divine aspiration. The inscriptions found on the statues provide valuable information about Antiochus's religious beliefs and his vision for the kingdom.
subtitle: Sanctuary of Gods and Kings

keyMyths.title: The Vision of Antiochus
keyMyths.description: Antiochus I Theos claimed divine lineage and sought to create a monument that would immortalize his name and connect him with the gods. His vision was to build a sanctuary that blended Greek and Persian religious traditions.
keyMyths.source: Historical accounts and inscriptions at Mount Nemrut

keyMyths.title: The Alignment with the Sun
keyMyths.description: The placement of the statues was carefully calculated to align with the rising and setting sun on specific dates, suggesting a deep understanding of astronomy and a reverence for the sun god. This alignment emphasizes the connection between the earthly and celestial realms.

sources.author: Theresa Goell
sources.work: Nemrud Dagi: The Throne of the Gods
sources.description: A comprehensive study of the archaeological excavations and historical significance of Mount Nemrut.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of the archaeological excavations and historical significance of Mount Nemrut.

sources.author: Donald H. Sanders
